examples.ja: 夏休みは楽しいです。
examples.furigana: 夏休[なつやす]みは楽[たの]しいです。

examples.ja: 夏休みに家族と旅行します。
examples.furigana: 夏休[なつやす]みに家族[かぞく]と旅行[りょこう]します。
examples.romaji: Natsu yasumi ni kazoku to ryokou shimasu.
examples.en: I will travel with my family during summer vacation.

components.note: interrogative pronoun for asking about things

何.etymology: The kanji 何 combines the person radical (亻) with 可 (possible/able), originally asking 'what is possible' or 'what can be.' This evolved into a general interrogative meaning 'what.' The character is used extensively in questions and has two main readings: なに (nani) when used alone, and なん (nan) when followed by certain sounds or counters.

examples.ja: これは何ですか。
examples.furigana: これは何[なん]ですか。

examples.ja: 何時に起きましたか。
examples.furigana: 何時[なんじ]に起[お]きましたか。
examples.en: What time did you wake up?

何か.etymology: 何 combines the person radical 亻 with 可 (possible/can), originally meaning 'what person' or 'what thing'. When combined with the particle か, it creates an indefinite pronoun meaning 'something' or 'anything'. This construction is common in Japanese for forming indefinite expressions.

examples.ja: 何か食べますか。
examples.furigana: 何[なに]か食[た]べますか。

examples.ja: 何か面白い本がありますか。
examples.furigana: 何[なに]か面白[おもしろ]い本[ほん]がありますか。
examples.romaji: Nanika omoshiroi hon ga arimasu ka.

components.note: Originally depicts a cutting or dividing motion, representing the number seven

七.etymology: 七 is one of the simplest Chinese numerals, consisting of a horizontal line with a bent stroke. The character represents the number seven and has been used consistently across Chinese and Japanese writing systems. In Japanese, it has both the on'yomi reading 'shichi' from Chinese and the native kun'yomi reading 'nana'.

examples.ja: 七時です。
examples.furigana: 七[なな]時[じ]です。

examples.ja: 七月は暑いです。
examples.furigana: 七[しち]月[がつ]は暑[あつ]いです。

components.note: Native Japanese counter for general objects

七つ.etymology: 七つ combines the kanji 七 (seven) with the native Japanese counter つ, which is used for counting general objects up to nine. This is the traditional Japanese way of counting, distinct from the Chinese-derived counting system. The つ counter is one of the most basic counting systems in Japanese.

examples.ja: りんごが七つあります。
examples.furigana: りんごが七[なな]つあります。

examples.ja: 七つの星が見えます。
examples.furigana: 七[なな]つの星[ほし]が見[み]えます。

components.note: Combines evening 夕 and mouth 口, representing calling out in the darkness
Shows a foot and a knife, meaning to go forward or in front

名前.etymology: 名 originally depicted calling out someone's name in the evening darkness, combining 夕 (evening) and 口 (mouth). 前 shows forward movement. Together, 名前 literally means 'name-front' or the name that goes before a person. This compound emerged in Japanese to specifically refer to personal names, distinguishing from other types of names or titles.

examples.ja: 私の名前は田中です。
examples.furigana: 私[わたし]の名前[なまえ]は田中[たなか]です。
examples.romaji: Watashi no namae wa Tanaka desu.

examples.ja: あなたの名前を教えてください。
examples.furigana: あなたの名前[なまえ]を教[おし]えてください。
examples.romaji: Anata no namae wo oshiete kudasai.

components.note: Shows two people standing side by side
Okurigana that forms the verb ending

並ぶ.etymology: 並 depicts two people standing side by side, representing the concept of lining up or being arranged in order. The character visually shows parallelism and orderly arrangement. In Japanese, 並ぶ means to form a line or queue, while 並べる means to arrange things in a line. This verb is fundamental for describing orderly arrangements in Japanese society.

examples.ja: みんなで並びます。
examples.furigana: みんなで並[なら]びます。

examples.ja: 店の前に人が並んでいます。
examples.furigana: 店[みせ]の前[まえ]に人[ひと]が並[なら]んでいます。
examples.romaji: Mise no mae ni hito ga narande imasu.
examples.en: People are lining up in front of the store.

なる.etymology: なる is a native Japanese verb written entirely in hiragana, meaning 'to become' or 'to turn into'. It expresses change of state or transformation and is one of the most fundamental verbs in Japanese grammar.

examples.ja: 大きくなります。
examples.furigana: 大[おお]きくなります。

examples.ja: 先生になりたいです。
examples.furigana: 先生[せんせい]になりたいです。
examples.en: I want to become a teacher.

components.note: main kanji expressing the action of throwing

投げる.etymology: The kanji 投 combines the hand radical 手 (扌) with 殳, representing a weapon or tool held in the hand. This pictographically represents the action of throwing or casting something with one's hand. The verb form 投げる uses the ichidan conjugation pattern common in Japanese verbs.

examples.ja: ボールを投げます。
examples.furigana: ボールを投[な]げます。

examples.ja: 石を川に投げました。
examples.furigana: 石[いし]を川[かわ]に投[な]げました。
examples.en: I threw a stone into the river.

components.note: main kanji expressing length or duration

長い.etymology: The kanji 長 originally depicted long hair flowing down, representing the concept of length. In ancient Chinese pictographs, it showed hair extending downward from a head. This meaning expanded to describe anything long in dimension or duration. As a Japanese i-adjective, it takes the ending い.

examples.ja: この道は長いです。
examples.furigana: この道[みち]は長[なが]いです。

examples.ja: 長い時間待ちました。
examples.furigana: 長[なが]い時間[じかん]待[ま]ちました。
examples.en: I waited for a long time.

components.note: depicts a vertical line through the center of a rectangle

中.etymology: The kanji 中 is a pictograph showing a vertical line passing through the center of a rectangular shape, representing the middle or center of something. This character is shared with Chinese and has maintained the same basic meaning across both languages. It's one of the most fundamental positional concepts in Japanese.

examples.ja: 箱の中にあります。
examples.furigana: 箱[はこ]の中[なか]にあります。

examples.ja: 学校の中は静かです。
examples.furigana: 学校[がっこう]の中[なか]は静[しず]かです。
examples.romaji: gakkou no naka wa shizuka desu

components.note: combines water radical with standing person

泣く.etymology: The kanji 泣 combines the water radical 氵(水) on the left with 立 (to stand) on the right, literally depicting tears (water) flowing from a standing person. This pictographic combination directly represents the act of crying or weeping. The verb 泣く follows the godan conjugation pattern.

examples.ja: 赤ちゃんが泣いています。
examples.furigana: 赤[あか]ちゃんが泣[な]いています。

examples.ja: 悲しくて泣きました。
examples.furigana: 悲[かな]しくて泣[な]きました。
examples.en: I cried because I was sad.

components.meaning: to lose, to die, to perish
components.note: main kanji expressing loss or death

亡くす.etymology: The kanji 亡 originally depicted a person hiding or disappearing, representing loss or death. It shows a simplified human figure that is partially concealed or missing. In Chinese tradition, this character represents the concept of perishing or being lost. The verb form 亡くす indicates causing something to be lost or to lose something precious.

examples.ja: 鍵を亡くしました。
examples.furigana: 鍵[かぎ]を亡[な]くしました。

examples.ja: 大切な友達を亡くしました。
examples.furigana: 大切[たいせつ]な友達[ともだち]を亡[な]くしました。

components.note: okurigana forming intransitive verb meaning 'to become without'

無くなる.etymology: The kanji 無 originally depicted dancers with ornamental feathers, but through semantic evolution came to mean 'nothing' or 'without' - perhaps representing the ephemeral nature of dance. This character is shared with Chinese and expresses complete absence or nonexistence. The verb 無くなる means 'to become nothing' or 'to disappear completely'.

examples.ja: お金が無くなりました。
examples.furigana: お金[かね]が無[な]くなりました。

examples.ja: 痛みが無くなりました。
examples.furigana: 痛[いた]みが無[な]くなりました。

肉.etymology: Originally depicted flesh or meat with lines showing the texture inside. The character combines the flesh radical (月) with interior markings. In ancient Chinese writing, this character was used to represent animal flesh used as food. The Japanese usage follows the Chinese meaning directly.

examples.ja: 肉を食べます。
examples.furigana: 肉[にく]を食[た]べます。

examples.ja: この肉は美味しいです。
examples.furigana: この肉[にく]は美味[おい]しいです。

components.note: ancient pictograph of a bird's nest or bag, associated with sunset direction

西.etymology: Originally a pictograph showing a bird settling into its nest at sunset, representing the west where the sun sets. The character resembles a bag or container with something inside. In ancient Chinese culture, west was associated with the end of day and settling down. Japanese adopted both the character and meaning from Chinese.

examples.ja: 西に行きます。
examples.furigana: 西[にし]に行[い]きます。

examples.ja: 太陽は西に沈みます。
examples.furigana: 太陽[たいよう]は西[にし]に沈[しず]みます。
examples.en: The sun sets in the west.

components.note: two horizontal strokes representing the number two

二.etymology: One of the simplest Chinese characters, consisting of two horizontal strokes to represent the number two. This is a direct pictographic representation where two lines equal two units. The character has remained virtually unchanged since ancient Chinese writing and was adopted into Japanese with the same meaning and basic pronunciation.

examples.ja: 二人です。
examples.furigana: 二人[ふたり]です。

examples.ja: 二時に会いましょう。
examples.furigana: 二時[にじ]に会[あ]いましょう。

二十.etymology: A compound number formed by combining 二 (two) and 十 (ten), literally meaning 'two tens' or twenty. This follows the Chinese decimal counting system where numbers are built logically by combining smaller units. The compound was adopted into Japanese both for mathematical counting and age designation.

examples.ja: 二十歳です。
examples.furigana: 二十歳[はたち]です。

examples.ja: 二十円ください。
examples.furigana: 二十円[にじゅうえん]ください。

components.note: hiragana native Japanese counter for objects

二つ.etymology: Combines the kanji 二 (two) with the native Japanese counter つ (tsu) to create a counting word for objects. This represents the Japanese native counting system (yamato kotoba) as opposed to the Chinese-derived system. The つ counter is used for small, discrete objects and is one of the most basic counting patterns in Japanese.

examples.ja: りんごを二つください。
examples.furigana: りんごを二[ふた]つください。

examples.ja: 二つの問題があります。
examples.furigana: 二[ふた]つの問題[もんだい]があります。

components.note: grass radical + what/why, originally meaning carried burden
specifies the load as objects or belongings

荷物.etymology: A compound word combining 荷 (load/burden) and 物 (things/objects). The first character 荷 originally depicted plants or materials that could be bundled and carried. Together, the compound specifically refers to personal belongings or cargo that is transported. This usage developed in Chinese and was adopted into Japanese for the same meaning of portable possessions.

examples.ja: 荷物を持ちます。
examples.furigana: 荷物[にもつ]を持[も]ちます。

examples.ja: 重い荷物がたくさんあります。
examples.furigana: 重[おも]い荷物[にもつ]がたくさんあります。
examples.en: There is a lot of heavy luggage.

人気.etymology: 人気 combines 人 (person) and 気 (spirit/energy) to mean 'the spirit or mood of people.' The character 人 depicts a standing human figure in profile. The compound originally referred to the general mood or atmosphere among people, which evolved to mean popularity or favor with people.

examples.ja: この歌手は人気です。
examples.furigana: この歌手[かしゅ]は人気[にんき]です。

examples.ja: 人気のレストランに行きました。
examples.furigana: 人気[にんき]のレストランに行[い]きました。
examples.en: I went to a popular restaurant.

components.note: represents the sun or origin of the sun

日本.etymology: 日本 literally means 'sun origin' or 'origin of the sun,' referring to Japan's position east of China. The character 日 originally depicted the sun as a circle with a dot in the center. 本 shows a tree with its roots marked, indicating the base or origin of something. Together they form the name for Japan as the land where the sun rises.

examples.ja: 私は日本人です。
examples.furigana: 私[わたし]は日本人[にほんじん]です。

examples.ja: 日本の文化は面白いです。
examples.furigana: 日本[にほん]の文化[ぶんか]は面白[おもしろ]いです。
examples.romaji: Nihon no bunka wa omoshiroi desu.

日本人.etymology: 日本人 combines 日本 (Japan, meaning 'origin of the sun') with 人 (person) to mean 'Japanese person.' This follows the common pattern in Japanese of adding 人 to country names to indicate nationality. The word specifically refers to people from Japan, distinguishing them from other nationalities.

examples.ja: 田中さんは日本人です。
examples.furigana: 田中[たなか]さんは日本人[にほんじん]です。

examples.ja: この学校には日本人の学生が多いです。
examples.furigana: この学校[がっこう]には日本人[にほんじん]の学生[がくせい]が多[おお]いです。
examples.romaji: Kono gakkou ni wa nihonjin no gakusei ga ooi desu.
examples.en: There are many Japanese students at this school.

日本語.etymology: 日本語 combines 日本 (Japan) with 語 (language) to mean 'Japanese language.' The character 語 contains the speech radical 言, indicating its connection to language and communication. This follows the standard pattern of adding 語 to country names to specify their languages, such as 英語 (English) or 中国語 (Chinese).

examples.ja: 私は日本語を勉強します。
examples.furigana: 私[わたし]は日本語[にほんご]を勉強[べんきょう]します。
examples.romaji: Watashi wa nihongo wo benkyou shimasu.

examples.ja: 日本語の漢字は難しいです。
examples.furigana: 日本語[にほんご]の漢字[かんじ]は難[むずか]しいです。
examples.romaji: Nihongo no kanji wa muzukashii desu.

components.note: indicates a structure or enclosed space
provides the sound and suggests an organized space

庭.etymology: The character 庭 consists of the building radical 广 and 廷 (court). Originally it referred to the courtyard of a government building or palace. The character evolved to mean any organized outdoor space attached to a building, particularly a garden. The combination suggests a cultivated outdoor area associated with a dwelling or structure.

examples.ja: 家の庭に花があります。
examples.furigana: 家[いえ]の庭[にわ]に花[はな]があります。
examples.romaji: Ie no niwa ni hana ga arimasu.
examples.en: There are flowers in the house's garden.

examples.ja: 毎朝庭で運動しています。
examples.furigana: 毎朝[まいあさ]庭[にわ]で運動[うんどう]しています。
examples.romaji: Maiasa niwa de undou shite imasu.
examples.en: I exercise in the garden every morning.

components.note: indicates this is a type of bird
provides phonetic component and suggests domestication

鶏.etymology: The character 鶏 combines 鳥 (bird) with 奚, which originally meant servant or domestic helper. This reflects the chicken's role as a domesticated bird that serves humans by providing eggs and meat. The character specifically distinguishes the domestic chicken from wild birds. In Japanese, it's read as にわとり, literally meaning 'garden bird,' emphasizing its domestic nature.

examples.ja: 鶏が庭にいます。
examples.furigana: 鶏[にわとり]が庭[にわ]にいます。
examples.en: There are chickens in the garden.

examples.ja: この鶏の卵は新鮮です。
examples.furigana: この鶏[にわとり]の卵[たまご]は新鮮[しんせん]です。
examples.romaji: Kono niwatori no tamago wa shinsen desu.

ニュース.etymology: A loanword from English 'news', adopted into Japanese and written in katakana. Commonly used in modern Japanese media and conversation.

examples.ja: ニュースを見ます。
examples.furigana: ニュースを見[み]ます。

examples.ja: 今日のニュースは面白かったです。
examples.furigana: 今日[きょう]のニュースは面白[おもしろ]かったです。
examples.romaji: kyou no nyuusu wa omoshirokatta desu

ノート.etymology: A loanword from English 'note', referring to a notebook or notepad. Written in katakana as a foreign borrowing.

examples.ja: ノートに書きます。
examples.furigana: ノートに書[か]きます。

examples.ja: 新しいノートを買いました。
examples.furigana: 新[あたら]しいノートを買[か]いました。

components.note: Main kanji representing the action of climbing

登る.etymology: The kanji 登 depicts feet (癶) moving upward, symbolizing climbing or ascending. Originally from Chinese, it represents the action of going up mountains or stairs. The character combines the radical for footsteps with elements suggesting upward movement.

examples.ja: 山に登ります。
examples.furigana: 山[やま]に登[のぼ]ります。

examples.ja: 階段を登るのは大変です。
examples.furigana: 階段[かいだん]を登[のぼ]るのは大変[たいへん]です。
examples.romaji: kaidan wo noboru no wa taihen desu

components.note: Radical indicating body part related to mouth/eating
Provides phonetic component for the reading

喉.etymology: The kanji 喉 combines the mouth radical 口 with 侯 (marquis), which provides the phonetic element. The character specifically refers to the throat area. In Chinese tradition, this character represented the passage for food and air, essential for life and speech.

examples.ja: 喉が痛いです。
examples.furigana: 喉[のど]が痛[いた]いです。

examples.ja: 風邪で喉の調子が悪いです。
examples.furigana: 風邪[かぜ]で喉[のど]の調子[ちょうし]が悪[わる]いです。
examples.romaji: kaze de nodo no choushi ga warui desu
examples.en: My throat condition is bad due to a cold.

components.note: Main verb expressing the state of thirst
Okurigana completing the verb 渇く

喉が渇く.etymology: This phrase combines 喉 (throat) with 渇く (to become dry/thirsty). The kanji 渇 uses the water radical 氵, indicating the absence or need for water. Together they form the common expression for being thirsty, literally meaning 'throat becomes dry'.

examples.ja: 喉が渇きました。
examples.furigana: 喉[のど]が渇[かわ]きました。

examples.ja: 運動した後で喉が渇いています。
examples.furigana: 運動[うんどう]した後[あと]で喉[のど]が渇[かわ]いています。
examples.romaji: undou shita ato de nodo ga kawaiteimasu

components.note: Verb stem meaning to ride or get on

乗り物.etymology: This compound word combines 乗る (to ride) with 物 (thing), literally meaning 'thing to ride on'. The kanji 乗 originally depicted a person climbing into a vehicle. Together they form the general term for any type of transportation vehicle.

examples.ja: 乗り物に乗ります。
examples.furigana: 乗[の]り物[もの]に乗[の]ります。

examples.ja: どんな乗り物が好きですか。
examples.furigana: どんな乗[の]り物[もの]が好[す]きですか。
examples.romaji: donna norimono ga suki desu ka
examples.en: What kind of vehicle do you like?

残る.etymology: The kanji 残 combines the radical 歹 (death, bone) with 戔 (small, narrow). Originally depicted bones remaining after death, symbolizing what is left behind. In Japanese, it developed the meaning of remaining or being left over in general contexts.

examples.ja: お金が残ります。
examples.furigana: お金[かね]が残[のこ]ります。

examples.ja: 時間が少し残っています。
examples.furigana: 時間[じかん]が少[すこ]し残[のこ]っています。

残す.etymology: Uses the same kanji 残 as 残る but with causative okurigana す, making it a transitive verb meaning to cause something to remain. The kanji originally depicted bones remaining after death, but evolved to mean leaving something behind intentionally or unintentionally.

examples.ja: 食べ物を残します。
examples.furigana: 食[た]べ物[もの]を残[のこ]します。

examples.ja: 宿題を明日まで残しました。
examples.furigana: 宿題[しゅくだい]を明日[あした]まで残[のこ]しました。

飲み物.etymology: Compound word combining 飲む (to drink) in its stem form with 物 (thing). The kanji 飲 shows the food radical 食 with 欠 (lack, open mouth), representing the act of consuming liquids. This creates the meaning of 'things for drinking' or beverages.

examples.ja: 飲み物を買います。
examples.furigana: 飲[の]み物[もの]を買[か]います。

examples.ja: 冷たい飲み物が欲しいです。
examples.furigana: 冷[つめ]たい飲[の]み物[もの]が欲[ほ]しいです。

飲む.etymology: The kanji 飲 combines the food radical 食 with 欠 (yawn, open mouth). This represents the action of consuming liquids through an open mouth. The character is shared with Chinese and has maintained its basic meaning across both languages.

examples.ja: 水を飲みます。
examples.furigana: 水[みず]を飲[の]みます。

examples.ja: 毎朝コーヒーを飲んでいます。
examples.furigana: 毎朝[まいあさ]コーヒーを飲[の]んでいます。

ノック.etymology: A katakana loanword from English 'knock'. Entered Japanese during the modern period as Western-style doors and knocking customs were adopted.

examples.ja: ドアのノックが聞こえます。
examples.furigana: ドアのノックが聞[き]こえます。
examples.en: I can hear a knock on the door.

examples.ja: 三回ノックをしてください。
examples.furigana: 三回[さんかい]ノックをしてください。

ノックする.etymology: Compound verb combining the English loanword ノック (knock) with the Japanese auxiliary verb する (to do), creating the action form of knocking.

examples.ja: ドアをノックします。
examples.furigana: ドアをノックします。

examples.ja: 入る前にノックしてください。
examples.furigana: 入[はい]る前[まえ]にノックしてください。
examples.romaji: Hairu mae ni nokku shite kudasai.

のんびり.etymology: A native Japanese adverb expressing a relaxed, unhurried state. The word mimics the slow, gentle pace of leisurely activities through its sound pattern.

examples.ja: 今日はのんびりします。
examples.furigana: 今日[きょう]はのんびりします。

examples.ja: 公園でのんびり散歩しました。
examples.furigana: 公園[こうえん]でのんびり散歩[さんぽ]しました。
examples.en: I took a leisurely walk in the park.

お.etymology: A native Japanese honorific prefix used to show respect or politeness. It is attached to nouns to make speech more polite and respectful.

examples.ja: お水をください。
examples.furigana: お水[みず]をください。

examples.ja: お名前を教えてください。
examples.furigana: お名前[なまえ]を教[おし]えてください。

components.note: represents the aesthetic quality of good taste
represents the sensory experience of eating

美味しい.etymology: Compound of 美 (beauty) and 味 (taste/flavor). The character 美 originally depicted a large sheep, symbolizing something fine or beautiful. Combined with 味, it creates the meaning of 'beautiful taste' or delicious. This word exemplifies how Japanese combines kanji with native grammatical elements.

examples.ja: このケーキは美味しいです。
examples.furigana: このケーキは美味[おい]しいです。

examples.ja: 母の料理はいつも美味しいです。
examples.furigana: 母[はは]の料理[りょうり]はいつも美味[おい]しいです。
examples.romaji: haha no ryouri wa itsumo oishii desu
examples.en: My mother's cooking is always delicious.

お疲れ.etymology: The kanji 疲 consists of the illness radical 疒 and 皮 (skin), originally suggesting the wearing away of the body like worn skin. With the honorific prefix お, this becomes a polite way to acknowledge someone's tiredness or hard work. Often used as a greeting among coworkers.

examples.ja: お疲れさまでした。
examples.furigana: お疲[つか]れさまでした。
examples.en: Thank you for your hard work.

examples.ja: 今日は本当にお疲れでしょう。
examples.furigana: 今日[きょう]は本当[ほんとう]にお疲[つか]れでしょう。
examples.romaji: kyou wa hontou ni otsukare deshou
examples.en: You must be really tired today.

components.note: represents the body part used for washing

お手洗い.etymology: Literally means 'hand-washing place.' The kanji 手 depicts a human hand, while 洗 combines water 氵 with 先 (first/previous), suggesting the primary act of cleaning. This euphemistic term for toilet reflects Japanese cultural emphasis on cleanliness and polite language.

examples.ja: お手洗いはどこですか。
examples.furigana: お手洗[てあら]いはどこですか。

examples.ja: お手洗いを借りてもいいですか。
examples.furigana: お手洗[てあら]いを借[か]りてもいいですか。
examples.romaji: otearai wo karite mo ii desu ka

お米.etymology: The kanji 米 pictographically represents rice grains scattered in different directions, showing the essential grain crop. With the honorific prefix お, it becomes the polite term for uncooked rice. Rice holds special cultural significance in Japan, hence the respectful prefix is commonly used.

examples.ja: お米を買いました。
examples.furigana: お米[こめ]を買[か]いました。

examples.ja: このお米はとても美味しいです。
examples.furigana: このお米[こめ]はとても美味[おい]しいです。
examples.romaji: kono okome wa totemo oishii desu

components.note: depicts layers stacked on top of each other

重い.etymology: The character 重 originally depicted objects stacked or layered on top of each other, conveying the concept of weight and heaviness. In Chinese tradition, multiple layers suggested both physical weight and metaphorical importance. The character combines the idea of accumulation with burden, leading to meanings of heaviness, importance, and repetition.

examples.ja: この本は重いです。
examples.furigana: この本[ほん]は重[おも]いです。

examples.ja: 重い荷物を運びました。
examples.furigana: 重[おも]い荷物[にもつ]を運[はこ]びました。

components.note: combines thread (糸) and winter (冬) suggesting completion

終わる.etymology: The kanji 終 combines 糸 (thread) and 冬 (winter). The concept relates to the end of spinning thread or the completion of work before winter arrives. In Chinese character development, this suggested the natural conclusion or termination of activities. The character evolved to represent any kind of ending or completion.

examples.ja: 授業が終わりました。
examples.furigana: 授業[じゅぎょう]が終[お]わりました。

examples.ja: 映画は何時に終わりますか。
examples.furigana: 映画[えいが]は何時[なんじ]に終[お]わりますか。
examples.romaji: eiga wa nanji ni owarimasu ka.
examples.en: What time does the movie end?

ペット.etymology: ペット is a loanword from English 'pet', adopted into Japanese using katakana script. It entered Japanese vocabulary during the modern period as Western concepts of companion animals became more common.

examples.ja: 犬はいいペットです。
examples.furigana: 犬[いぬ]はいいペットです。

examples.ja: どんなペットを飼っていますか。
examples.furigana: どんなペットを飼[か]っていますか。
examples.romaji: donna petto wo katte imasu ka.
examples.en: What kind of pet do you have?

プレゼント.etymology: プレゼント is a loanword from English 'present', written in katakana. It was adopted into Japanese to describe the Western concept of gift-giving, particularly for birthdays and holidays.

examples.ja: 誕生日にプレゼントをもらいました。
examples.furigana: 誕生日[たんじょうび]にプレゼントをもらいました。
examples.en: I received a present on my birthday.

examples.ja: 友達にプレゼントを買いました。
examples.furigana: 友達[ともだち]にプレゼントを買[か]いました。
examples.en: I bought a present for my friend.

components.note: represents the lunar cycle and calendar month

来月.etymology: 来月 combines 来 (come/next) and 月 (month). The character 来 originally depicted wheat or grain, suggesting something that arrives or comes forth. 月 represents the moon and by extension the monthly cycle. Together they form the concept of the month that is coming or approaching.

examples.ja: 来月、日本に行きます。
examples.furigana: 来月[らいげつ]、日本[にほん]に行[い]きます。
examples.en: I will go to Japan next month.

examples.ja: 来月の予定を教えてください。
examples.furigana: 来月[らいげつ]の予定[よてい]を教[おし]えてください。
examples.romaji: raigetsu no yotei wo oshiete kudasai.
examples.en: Please tell me next month's schedule.

リンゴ.etymology: リンゴ comes from the Ainu language word 'ringo' meaning apple. It was adopted into Japanese and is typically written in katakana, though the kanji 林檎 also exists but is rarely used in modern Japanese.

examples.ja: リンゴを食べます。
examples.furigana: リンゴを食[た]べます。

examples.ja: 赤いリンゴが好きです。
examples.furigana: 赤[あか]いリンゴが好[す]きです。

両親.etymology: This compound combines 両 (both) and 親 (parent) to mean 'both parents'. The character 両 originally depicted a balance or scale, representing the concept of 'both sides'. The character 親 shows a person (亲) looking closely (見), representing the intimate relationship of a parent. Together they specifically refer to both mother and father as a unit.

examples.ja: 両親は元気です。
examples.furigana: 両親[りょうしん]は元気[げんき]です。

examples.ja: 両親と一緒に映画を見ました。
examples.furigana: 両親[りょうしん]と一緒[いっしょ]に映画[えいが]を見[み]ました。
examples.romaji: Ryoushin to issho ni eiga wo mimashita.
examples.en: I watched a movie together with my parents.

components.note: represents traveling or going on a journey
indicates the action of going or moving

旅行.etymology: This compound combines 旅 (journey) and 行 (go) to mean travel or trip. The character 旅 originally showed people with flags traveling together. The character 行 depicts a crossroads, representing movement and going. This word emphasizes the act of going on a journey for pleasure or business.

examples.ja: 来月、京都に旅行します。
examples.furigana: 来月[らいげつ]、京都[きょうと]に旅行[りょこう]します。
examples.en: Next month, I will travel to Kyoto.

examples.ja: 友達と海外旅行の計画を立てています。
examples.furigana: 友達[ともだち]と海外[かいがい]旅行[りょこう]の計画[けいかく]を立[た]てています。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi to kaigai ryokou no keikaku wo tatete imasu.
examples.en: I'm making plans for an overseas trip with my friends.

留学生.onyomi: リュウ / ガク / セイ
留学生.kunyomi: と.める / まな.ぶ / い.きる
留学生.etymology: This compound combines 留 (stay/remain), 学 (study), and 生 (student) to mean exchange student. The character 留 shows keeping something in place. The character 学 depicts a child learning under a roof. The character 生 represents new growth or life. Together they describe a student who stays in a foreign country to study.

examples.ja: 私は日本の留学生です。
examples.furigana: 私[わたし]は日本[にほん]の留学生[りゅうがくせい]です。
examples.romaji: Watashi wa Nihon no ryuugakusei desu.
examples.en: I am an exchange student in Japan.

examples.ja: 大学に留学生がたくさんいます。
examples.furigana: 大学[だいがく]に留学生[りゅうがくせい]がたくさんいます。
examples.romaji: Daigaku ni ryuugakusei ga takusan imasu.
examples.en: There are many exchange students at the university.

寂しい.etymology: This word uses the kanji 寂 with the adjective ending しい. The character 寂 combines the roof radical 宀 (house) with 叔 (uncle), originally meaning quiet or still. In Chinese, this character also means lonely or desolate. The adjective form さびしい expresses the emotional state of loneliness or feeling isolated from others.

examples.ja: 一人で寂しいです。
examples.furigana: 一人[ひとり]で寂[さび]しいです。

examples.ja: 友達がいなくて寂しい気持ちになりました。
examples.furigana: 友達[ともだち]がいなくて寂[さび]しい気持[きも]ちになりました。
examples.romaji: Tomodachi ga inakute sabishii kimochi ni narimashita.
examples.en: I felt lonely because I had no friends.

components.note: indicates something that comes before in time
indicates a measure of time or distance

先程.etymology: This compound combines 先 (before/ahead) and 程 (extent/degree) to mean 'a little while ago'. The character 先 shows a person walking ahead, representing precedence in time or space. The character 程 combines grain and rule, indicating measurement or extent. Together they create a time expression referring to the recent past.

examples.ja: 先程はありがとうございました。
examples.furigana: 先程[さきほど]はありがとうございました。

examples.ja: 先程お話しした件について質問があります。
examples.furigana: 先程[さきほど]お話[はな]しした件[けん]について質問[しつもん]があります。
examples.romaji: Sakihodo ohanashi shita ken ni tsuite shitsumon ga arimasu.
examples.en: I have a question about the matter we discussed a little while ago.

components.note: represents fermentation vessel or alcohol container

酒.etymology: The character 酒 combines the water radical 氵 and 酉 (wine jar/rooster). The component 酉 originally depicted a wine jar used for fermentation and later came to represent the zodiac rooster. This character is shared with Chinese and has the same meaning of alcoholic beverages. In Japanese, it refers to both sake (rice wine) and alcohol in general.

examples.ja: お酒を飲みません。
examples.furigana: お酒[さけ]を飲[の]みません。

examples.ja: 日本酒は米から作られます。
examples.furigana: 日本酒[にほんしゅ]は米[こめ]から作[つく]られます。
examples.en: Japanese sake is made from rice.

最初.etymology: This compound combines 最 (most, extreme) with 初 (first, beginning). The character 最 originally depicted taking something with the hand to the utmost degree, while 初 shows a knife cutting cloth, representing the beginning of making clothes. Together they emphasize the very first or earliest point in time or sequence.

examples.ja: 最初に手を洗います。
examples.furigana: 最初[さいしょ]に手[て]を洗[あら]います。

examples.ja: 最初はとても難しかったです。
examples.furigana: 最初[さいしょ]はとても難[むずか]しかったです。
examples.en: It was very difficult at first.

components.note: indicates final position in time or space

最後.etymology: This compound combines 最 (most, extreme) with 後 (after, behind). The character 後 originally showed a person walking with the foot lagging behind, representing the concept of 'after' or 'behind'. Combined with 最, it creates the meaning of the very last or final point in a sequence.

examples.ja: 最後に質問があります。
examples.furigana: 最後[さいご]に質問[しつもん]があります。
examples.en: I have a question at the end.

examples.ja: これが最後のチャンスです。
examples.furigana: これが最後[さいご]のチャンスです。
examples.romaji: Kore ga saigo no chansu desu.

しかし.etymology: This conjunction word is written entirely in hiragana and serves as a formal way to express contrast or contradiction between statements, similar to 'however' or 'but' in English.

examples.ja: 天気はいいです。しかし、寒いです。
examples.furigana: 天気[てんき]はいいです。しかし、寒[さむ]いです。
examples.romaji: Tenki wa ii desu. Shikashi, samui desu.
examples.en: The weather is nice. However, it's cold.

examples.ja: 勉強しました。しかし、テストは難しかったです。
examples.furigana: 勉強[べんきょう]しました。しかし、テストは難[むずか]しかったです。
examples.romaji: Benkyou shimashita. Shikashi, tesuto wa muzukashikatta desu.
examples.en: I studied. However, the test was difficult.

白い.etymology: The character 白 represents the color white and is thought to derive from the image of a white bean or acorn. In ancient Chinese writing, it depicted something bright and clear. The character serves as its own radical and forms the basis for many words related to brightness, clarity, and the color white.

examples.ja: 雪は白いです。
examples.furigana: 雪[ゆき]は白[しろ]いです。

examples.ja: 白いシャツを着ています。
examples.furigana: 白[しろ]いシャツを着[き]ています。
examples.en: I am wearing a white shirt.

静か.etymology: The character 静 combines the radical 青 (blue/green) with 争 (conflict). The original meaning suggests the calm blue-green color that comes after conflict has ceased, representing tranquility and quietness. This kanji is shared with Chinese and carries the same meaning of stillness and peace.

examples.ja: 図書館は静かです。
examples.furigana: 図書館[としょかん]は静[しず]かです。

examples.ja: 静かな公園で読書をしました。
examples.furigana: 静[しず]かな公園[こうえん]で読書[どくしょ]をしました。
examples.romaji: Shizuka na kouen de dokusho wo shimashita.
examples.en: I read a book in the quiet park.

components.note: indicates expanding one's presence or activities

出張.etymology: This compound combines 出 (to go out, exit) with 張 (to stretch, extend). The character 出 shows something emerging from an enclosure, while 張 originally depicted stretching a bow. Together they create the concept of extending one's business activities by going out to distant locations, hence 'business trip'.

examples.ja: 来週、東京に出張します。
examples.furigana: 来週[らいしゅう]、東京[とうきょう]に出張[しゅっちょう]します。
examples.en: Next week, I will go on a business trip to Tokyo.

examples.ja: 出張から帰ってきました。
examples.furigana: 出張[しゅっちょう]から帰[かえ]ってきました。
examples.en: I came back from a business trip.

小説.etymology: 小説 combines 小 (small) and 説 (explanation, story). Originally meant 'small talk' or 'trivial stories' in Chinese literature. The term was adopted in Japanese to refer to fictional narrative works, distinguishing them from historical or factual writings. The 'small' aspect referred to stories about ordinary people rather than grand historical events.

examples.ja: この小説はおもしろいです。
examples.furigana: この小説[しょうせつ]はおもしろいです。

examples.ja: 日本の小説を読んでいます。
examples.furigana: 日本[にほん]の小説[しょうせつ]を読[よ]んでいます。
examples.romaji: Nihon no shousetsu wo yonde imasu.
examples.en: I am reading a Japanese novel.

紹介.etymology: 紹介 combines 紹 (to connect, continue) and 介 (to intervene, mediate). The character 紹 contains the silk radical, suggesting the idea of connecting threads. 介 shows a person standing between others, acting as a mediator. Together, they express the concept of connecting people or bringing someone into a new situation through mediation.

examples.ja: 友達を紹介します。
examples.furigana: 友達[ともだち]を紹介[しょうかい]します。

examples.ja: 自己紹介をお願いします。
examples.furigana: 自己紹介[じこしょうかい]をお願[ねが]いします。

三日.english: three days / third day of month
三日.etymology: 三日 combines 三 (three) and 日 (day). The character 三 represents three horizontal lines, symbolizing the number three in Chinese numerals. 日 originally depicted the sun and came to mean day. When counting days in Japanese, special phonetic changes occur, with 三日 pronounced みっか rather than the expected さんにち, following ancient Japanese counting patterns.

examples.ja: 三日間休みます。
examples.furigana: 三日間[みっかかん]休[やす]みます。
examples.en: I will take a three-day break.

examples.ja: 今日は三月三日です。
examples.furigana: 今日[きょう]は三月[さんがつ]三日[みっか]です。

三つ.etymology: 三つ combines the kanji 三 (three) with the hiragana つ, which serves as a native Japanese counter for general objects. The character 三 shows three horizontal strokes representing the number three. This counting system (ひとつ, ふたつ, みっつ) represents the ancient Japanese number system, predating Chinese numerical influence, and is used for counting tangible objects up to ten.

examples.ja: りんごを三つください。
examples.furigana: りんごを三[みっ]つください。

examples.ja: 机の上に本が三つあります。
examples.furigana: 机[つくえ]の上[うえ]に本[ほん]が三[みっ]つあります。
examples.romaji: Tsukue no ue ni hon ga mittsu arimasu.
examples.en: There are three books on the desk.

components.note: bottom component, here serving structural role

皆.etymology: 皆 is composed of 白 (white, clear) at the top and 王 (king) at the bottom. The character originally meant 'complete' or 'all,' with the white component suggesting clarity and completeness. In Japanese, it developed the meaning of 'everyone' or 'all people.' The kun'yomi reading みな reflects the native Japanese word for 'all' or 'everyone,' commonly used in daily conversation.

examples.ja: 皆さん、おはようございます。
examples.furigana: 皆[みな]さん、おはようございます。

examples.ja: 皆で映画を見に行きました。
examples.furigana: 皆[みな]で映画[えいが]を見[み]に行[い]きました。
examples.romaji: Mina de eiga wo mi ni ikimashita.
examples.en: We all went to see a movie together.

南.etymology: 南 originally depicted a house or enclosure (冂) with decorative elements, possibly representing a dwelling facing the warm southern direction. The character evolved to specifically mean 'south' in Chinese writing systems. In ancient Chinese culture, south was associated with warmth, light, and positive energy. The kun'yomi reading みなみ is the native Japanese word for the southern direction, used alongside the Chinese-derived reading.

examples.ja: 南の方角です。
examples.furigana: 南[みなみ]の方角[ほうがく]です。

examples.ja: 南口で待っています。
examples.furigana: 南口[みなみぐち]で待[ま]っています。
examples.en: I am waiting at the south exit.

components.note: represents value or things of worth

貰う.etymology: The character 貰 combines the shell radical 貝 (representing money or valuable items) with 文 as a phonetic component. This kanji specifically developed to represent the act of receiving something from another person. The shell radical reinforces the concept of receiving items of value, making it distinct from other receiving verbs in Japanese.

examples.ja: プレゼントを貰いました。
examples.furigana: プレゼントを貰[もら]いました。

examples.ja: 友達から手紙を貰って嬉しかったです。
examples.furigana: 友達[ともだち]から手紙[てがみ]を貰[もら]って嬉[うれ]しかったです。
examples.romaji: tomodachi kara tegami o moratte ureshikatta desu.
examples.en: I was happy to receive a letter from my friend.

六日.english: six days / sixth day of month
六日.etymology: This compound word combines 六 (six) with 日 (day). The kanji 六 originally depicted a roof structure, symbolizing completeness and the number six in Chinese numerology. When combined with 日, it forms either 'six days' or 'the sixth day of the month'. The reading むいか is the native Japanese counter for this specific date and duration.

examples.ja: 今日は六日です。
examples.furigana: 今日[きょう]は六日[むいか]です。

examples.ja: 旅行は六日間でした。
examples.furigana: 旅行[りょこう]は六日[むいか]間[かん]でした。
examples.en: The trip was for six days.

components.note: native Japanese counter for small objects

六つ.etymology: This word combines the kanji 六 (six) with the hiragana counter つ, forming the native Japanese counting system for general objects. The kanji 六 derives from ancient Chinese and represents the number six through its structural form. The つ counter is used specifically for counting small, round, or general objects in Japanese, making むっつ the standard way to count six items.

examples.ja: りんごが六つあります。
examples.furigana: りんごが六[むっ]つあります。

examples.ja: 机の上にペンが六つ置いてあります。
examples.furigana: 机[つくえ]の上[うえ]にペンが六[むっ]つ置[お]いてあります。
examples.romaji: tsukue no ue ni pen ga muttsu oite arimasu.
examples.en: There are six pens placed on the desk.

components.note: the outer structure suggests facing or turning toward something

向く.etymology: The character 向 depicts a person facing toward a specific direction, with the 口 radical representing an opening or direction. This kanji originated in Chinese to express the concept of facing, turning toward, or heading in a particular direction. The character's structure visually suggests movement or orientation toward a target, making it naturally suited for expressing directional concepts in Japanese.

examples.ja: 窓の方を向いてください。
examples.furigana: 窓[まど]の方[ほう]を向[む]いてください。
examples.romaji: mado no hou o muite kudasai.

examples.ja: 彼は私の方向を向いて話しました。
examples.furigana: 彼[かれ]は私[わたし]の方向[ほうこう]を向[む]いて話[はな]しました。
examples.romaji: kare wa watashi no houkou o muite hanashimashita.
examples.en: He faced my direction and spoke.

無駄.etymology: This compound word combines 無 (nothing, without) and 駄 (worthless, inferior quality). The character 無 originally depicted dancers around a fire, later developing to mean 'nothing' or 'without.' The character 駄 combines the horse radical with a phonetic element, originally referring to a poor-quality horse, then extending to mean anything worthless or inferior. Together, 無駄 expresses the concept of wastefulness or futility.

examples.ja: 時間を無駄にしました。
examples.furigana: 時間[じかん]を無駄[むだ]にしました。

examples.ja: そんな無駄なことをしないでください。
examples.furigana: そんな無駄[むだ]なことをしないでください。
examples.romaji: sonna muda na koto o shinaide kudasai.
examples.en: Please don't do such wasteful things.

昨日.etymology: This compound word combines 昨 (past, previous) with 日 (day, sun). The character 昨 contains the sun radical 日 with additional components suggesting 'past' or 'previous,' derived from Chinese usage. The combination 昨日 literally means 'the previous day,' creating the concept of yesterday. This word demonstrates the Chinese influence on Japanese temporal expressions, though the reading きのう is purely Japanese.

examples.ja: 昨日は雨でした。
examples.furigana: 昨日[きのう]は雨[あめ]でした。

examples.ja: 昨日友達と映画を見に行きました。
examples.furigana: 昨日[きのう]友達[ともだち]と映画[えいが]を見[み]に行[い]きました。
examples.romaji: kinou tomodachi to eiga o mi ni ikimashita.
examples.en: Yesterday I went to see a movie with my friend.

サラダ.etymology: A loanword from English 'salad', written in katakana as is typical for foreign words in Japanese. The word entered Japanese during the Meiji period with Western culinary influences.

examples.ja: サラダを食べます。
examples.furigana: サラダを食[た]べます。

examples.ja: 野菜サラダが好きです。
examples.furigana: 野菜[やさい]サラダが好[す]きです。

さる.etymology: A native Japanese word written in hiragana. While the kanji 猿 exists for monkey, the hiragana form さる is commonly used in basic contexts.

examples.ja: さるがいます。
examples.furigana: さるがいます。

examples.ja: 動物園でさるを見ました。
examples.furigana: 動物園[どうぶつえん]でさるを見[み]ました。
examples.en: I saw monkeys at the zoo.

components.note: three horizontal strokes representing the number three

三.etymology: An ancient pictographic character showing three horizontal lines to represent the number three. This is one of the simplest Chinese numerals, directly depicting quantity through visual representation. The character has remained virtually unchanged throughout history and is used identically in Chinese and Japanese writing systems.

examples.ja: 三時です。
examples.furigana: 三時[さんじ]です。

examples.ja: 三人で映画を見ました。
examples.furigana: 三人[さんにん]で映画[えいが]を見[み]ました。
examples.en: Three of us watched a movie.

四.kunyomi: よっ.つ / よ / よん

components.note: enclosed area divided into parts representing four

四.etymology: Originally depicted as a mouth or enclosure (囗) with divisions inside representing the number four. The character evolved from ancient pictographic representations showing four distinct parts within a boundary. In Japanese, this kanji shares the same form and meaning as traditional Chinese, representing one of the fundamental numbers in East Asian counting systems.

examples.ja: 四月です。
examples.furigana: 四月[しがつ]です。

examples.ja: 四人家族です。
examples.furigana: 四人[よにん]家族[かぞく]です。
examples.en: We are a family of four.

下.kunyomi: した / しも / もと / さ.げる / さ.がる / くだ.る / くだ.す / お.りる / お.ろす

components.note: horizontal line with a short vertical line pointing downward

下.etymology: A simple pictographic character showing a horizontal reference line with a short stroke beneath it, indicating the concept of 'below' or 'under'. This character directly represents spatial relationships and is fundamental in both Chinese and Japanese writing. The visual representation makes the meaning immediately apparent through its downward-pointing structure.

examples.ja: 机の下にあります。
examples.furigana: 机[つくえ]の下[した]にあります。

examples.ja: 階段を下りました。
examples.furigana: 階段[かいだん]を下[お]りました。

シャツ.etymology: Borrowed from English "shirt" during the Meiji period. The katakana シャツ represents the phonetic adaptation of the English word into Japanese.

examples.ja: 白いシャツを着ています。
examples.furigana: 白[しろ]いシャツを着[き]ています。
examples.en: I am wearing a white shirt.

examples.ja: 新しいシャツを買いました。
examples.furigana: 新[あたら]しいシャツを買[か]いました。

シャワー.etymology: Borrowed from English "shower" in modern Japanese. The katakana シャワー phonetically represents the English pronunciation adapted to Japanese sound patterns.

examples.ja: 毎朝シャワーを浴びます。
examples.furigana: 毎朝[まいあさ]シャワーを浴[あ]びます。
examples.en: I take a shower every morning.

examples.ja: シャワーのお湯が熱すぎます。
examples.furigana: シャワーのお湯[ゆ]が熱[あつ]すぎます。
examples.en: The shower water is too hot.

components.note: represents a formal building or hall

食堂.etymology: A compound of 食 (food/eating) and 堂 (hall). Both kanji have Chinese origins. 食 originally depicted a food vessel with a lid, while 堂 represents a formal building. Together they form the concept of a dining hall or place where meals are served.

examples.ja: 学校の食堂で昼ご飯を食べます。
examples.furigana: 学校[がっこう]の食堂[しょくどう]で昼[ひる]ご飯[はん]を食[た]べます。
examples.romaji: gakkou no shokudou de hirugohan wo tabemasu
examples.en: I eat lunch in the school cafeteria.

examples.ja: 食堂のカレーライスはとても美味しいです。
examples.furigana: 食堂[しょくどう]のカレーライスはとても美味[おい]しいです。
examples.romaji: shokudou no karee raisu wa totemo oishii desu
examples.en: The curry rice in the cafeteria is very delicious.

components.note: pictograph of a white rice grain or acorn

白.etymology: Originally a pictograph representing a white rice grain or acorn. The character has ancient Chinese origins and represents the concept of whiteness and purity. In Japanese, it maintains both the Chinese-derived readings (ハク/ビャク) and the native Japanese reading (しろ).

examples.ja: 雪は白いです。
examples.furigana: 雪[ゆき]は白[しろ]いです。

examples.ja: 白い猫が庭で遊んでいます。
examples.furigana: 白[しろ]い猫[ねこ]が庭[にわ]で遊[あそ]んでいます。
examples.romaji: shiroi neko ga niwa de asonde imasu
examples.en: A white cat is playing in the garden.

すぐ.etymology: A native Japanese adverb meaning "immediately" or "soon." Written entirely in hiragana with no kanji representation in modern usage.

examples.ja: すぐ行きます。
examples.furigana: すぐ行[い]きます。

examples.ja: 家からすぐ近くに駅があります。
examples.furigana: 家[いえ]からすぐ近[ちか]くに駅[えき]があります。
examples.romaji: ie kara sugu chikaku ni eki ga arimasu
examples.en: There is a station very close to my house.

スリッパ.etymology: Borrowed from English "slipper" with the typical Japanese adaptation adding a vowel sound to end the word. Written in katakana as a foreign loanword.

examples.ja: 家でスリッパを履きます。
examples.furigana: 家[いえ]でスリッパを履[は]きます。

examples.ja: 玄関にスリッパが置いてあります。
examples.furigana: 玄関[げんかん]にスリッパが置[お]いてあります。
examples.romaji: genkan ni surippa ga oite arimasu
examples.en: Slippers are placed at the entrance.

背が高い.kunyomi: せ / せい / そむ.く / そむ.ける
背が高い.etymology: 背 originally depicted a person's back, combining 北 (back-to-back) and 月 (flesh/body). The character represents the back or spine of a person. When combined with 高い (tall), it forms the common expression for describing someone's height. This phrase literally means 'back/stature is tall.'

examples.ja: 田中さんは背が高いです。
examples.furigana: 田中[たなか]さんは背[せ]が高[たか]いです。
examples.romaji: Tanaka-san wa se ga takai desu.

examples.ja: あの人は背が高くて、よく目立ちます。
examples.furigana: あの人[ひと]は背[せ]が高[たか]くて、よく目立[めだ]ちます。
examples.romaji: Ano hito wa se ga takakute, yoku medachimasu.
examples.en: That person is tall and stands out well.

セーター.etymology: セーター is a loanword from English 'sweater,' written in katakana as is standard for foreign borrowings. It entered Japanese vocabulary during the modern period as Western clothing became popular.

examples.ja: 寒いので、セーターを着ます。
examples.furigana: 寒[さむ]いので、セーターを着[き]ます。
examples.romaji: Samui no de, seetaa wo kimasu.
examples.en: It's cold, so I'll wear a sweater.

examples.ja: このセーターは母が編んでくれました。
examples.furigana: このセーターは母[はは]が編[あ]んでくれました。
examples.romaji: Kono seetaa wa haha ga ande kuremashita.
examples.en: My mother knitted this sweater for me.

components.note: provides sound and meaning of narrowness

狭い.etymology: 狭 combines the dog radical 犭 with 夹 (pinch/squeeze). The character originally conveyed the idea of being pressed between or squeezed, like an animal in a tight space. In modern usage, it describes narrow spaces or small areas. The meaning evolved from the physical concept of being compressed or confined.

examples.ja: この部屋は狭いです。
examples.furigana: この部屋[へや]は狭[せま]いです。

examples.ja: 道が狭くて、車が通れません。
examples.furigana: 道[みち]が狭[せま]くて、車[くるま]が通[とお]れません。
examples.romaji: Michi ga semakute, kuruma ga tooremasen.
examples.en: The road is narrow and cars can't pass through.

先週.etymology: 先週 combines 先 (before/previous) and 週 (week). 先 originally showed a person's legs moving forward, representing 'going ahead' or 'previous.' 週 contains the walking radical, indicating a cycle of time. Together they form the concept of 'the week that came before,' referring to the previous seven-day period.

examples.ja: 先週は忙しかったです。
examples.furigana: 先週[せんしゅう]は忙[いそが]しかったです。

examples.ja: 先週の日曜日に映画を見ました。
examples.furigana: 先週[せんしゅう]の日曜日[にちようび]に映画[えいが]を見[み]ました。
examples.romaji: Senshuu no nichiyoubi ni eiga wo mimashita.
examples.en: I watched a movie last Sunday.

洗濯.etymology: 洗濯 combines two water-related characters: 洗 (wash) and 濯 (rinse). 洗 contains the water radical and elements suggesting the action of cleaning. 濯 also has the water radical with components indicating thorough cleansing. Together they represent the complete process of washing clothes or doing laundry, emphasizing both the initial washing and final rinsing stages.

examples.ja: 毎日洗濯をします。
examples.furigana: 毎日[まいにち]洗濯[せんたく]をします。

examples.ja: 洗濯物を干している間に、掃除をしました。
examples.furigana: 洗濯物[せんたくもの]を干[ほ]している間[あいだ]に、掃除[そうじ]をしました。
examples.romaji: Sentakumono wo hoshite iru aida ni, souji wo shimashita.
examples.en: I cleaned while the laundry was hanging to dry.

そして.etymology: そして is a native Japanese conjunction written entirely in hiragana. It connects sentences or clauses, indicating sequence or addition, similar to 'and then' or 'and' in English.

examples.ja: 朝ご飯を食べて、そして学校に行きます。
examples.furigana: 朝[あさ]ご飯[はん]を食[た]べて、そして学校[がっこう]に行[い]きます。
examples.romaji: Asagohan wo tabete, soshite gakkou ni ikimasu.
examples.en: I eat breakfast, and then go to school.

examples.ja: 宿題を終わらせて、そして友達と遊びに行きました。
examples.furigana: 宿題[しゅくだい]を終[お]わらせて、そして友達[ともだち]と遊[あそ]びに行[い]きました。
examples.romaji: Shukudai wo owarasete, soshite tomodachi to asobi ni ikimashita.
examples.en: I finished my homework, and then went to play with friends.

外.etymology: The character 外 depicts the concept of being outside or external. It combines the evening radical (夕) with a divination element (ト), possibly representing the time when activities move outside or the boundary between inside and outside spaces. This kanji is shared with Chinese, where it carries the same basic meaning of 'outside' or 'external'.

examples.ja: 外は寒いです。
examples.furigana: 外[そと]は寒[さむ]いです。

examples.ja: 外国人の友達がいます。
examples.furigana: 外国人[がいこくじん]の友達[ともだち]がいます。

その.etymology: その is a demonstrative pronoun in Japanese meaning 'that' (referring to something near the listener). It's part of the ko-so-a-do system of demonstratives in Japanese.

examples.ja: その本は面白いです。
examples.furigana: その本[ほん]は面白[おもしろ]いです。

examples.ja: その話をもう一度聞かせてください。
examples.furigana: その話[はなし]をもう一度[いちど]聞[き]かせてください。
examples.romaji: sono hanashi wo mou ichido kikasete kudasai
examples.en: Please tell me that story one more time.

それ.etymology: それ is a demonstrative pronoun meaning 'that thing' (referring to something near the listener or previously mentioned). It's part of the Japanese ko-so-a-do demonstrative system.

examples.ja: それは私の本です。
examples.furigana: それは私[わたし]の本[ほん]です。
examples.romaji: sore wa watashi no hon desu

examples.ja: それを買いたいと思います。
examples.furigana: それを買[か]いたいと思[おも]います。
examples.en: I think I want to buy that.

それで.etymology: それで is a conjunction combining それ (that) and で (particle indicating means or cause), literally meaning 'with that' or 'because of that', used to express logical consequence.

examples.ja: 雨でした。それで、家にいました。
examples.furigana: 雨[あめ]でした。それで、家[いえ]にいました。
examples.romaji: ame deshita. sore de, ie ni imashita
examples.en: It was raining. So, I stayed home.

examples.ja: 電車が遅れました。それで、会議に間に合いませんでした。
examples.furigana: 電車[でんしゃ]が遅[おく]れました。それで、会議[かいぎ]に間[ま]に合[あ]いませんでした。
examples.romaji: densha ga okuremashita. sore de, kaigi ni maniaimasen deshita
examples.en: The train was late. Therefore, I couldn't make it to the meeting.

すると.etymology: すると is a conjunction formed from the verb する (to do) plus the conditional particle と, meaning 'when one does that' or 'then'. It indicates a temporal sequence or immediate consequence.

examples.ja: ドアを開けました。すると、猫がいました。
examples.furigana: ドアを開[あ]けました。すると、猫[ねこ]がいました。
examples.romaji: doa wo akemashita. suru to, neko ga imashita
examples.en: I opened the door. Then, there was a cat.

examples.ja: ボタンを押しました。すると、機械が動き始めました。
examples.furigana: ボタンを押[お]しました。すると、機械[きかい]が動[うご]き始[はじ]めました。
examples.romaji: botan wo oshimashita. suru to, kikai ga ugoki hajimemashita
examples.en: I pressed the button. Thereupon, the machine started moving.

好き.etymology: The character 好 combines woman (女) and child (子), representing the natural affection between mother and child, extending to mean 'good', 'like', or 'love'. This kanji is shared with Chinese and carries similar meanings of preference and fondness. In Japanese, 好き is commonly used as a na-adjective to express liking something or someone.

examples.ja: 私は音楽が好きです。
examples.furigana: 私[わたし]は音楽[おんがく]が好[す]きです。
examples.romaji: watashi wa ongaku ga suki desu

examples.ja: どんな食べ物が好きですか。
examples.furigana: どんな食[た]べ物[もの]が好[す]きですか。
examples.romaji: donna tabemono ga suki desu ka
examples.en: What kind of food do you like?
